mn106:1.2: that on one occasion the Blessed One was staying among the Kurus. Now there is a town of the Kurus called Kammāsadhamma.
mn106:1.3: There the Blessed One addressed the monks,
mn106:1.5: “Yes, lord,” the monks responded to him.
mn106:2.1: “Monks, sensuality is inconstant, hollow, vain, deceptive.
mn106:2.2: It is illusory, the babble of fools.
mn106:2.3: Sensuality here & now; sensuality in lives to come;
mn106:2.4: sensual perceptions here & now; sensual perceptions in lives to come:
mn106:2.5: both are Māra’s realm, Māra’s domain, Māra’s bait, Māra’s range.
mn106:2.6: They lead to these evil, unskillful mental states: greed, ill will, & contentiousness.
mn106:2.7: They arise for the obstruction of a disciple of the noble ones here in training.
mn106:3.1: In that case, the disciple of the noble ones considers this:
mn106:3.2: ‘Sensuality here & now; sensuality in lives to come;
mn106:3.3: sensual perceptions here & now; sensual perceptions in lives to come:
mn106:3.4: Both are Māra’s realm, Māra’s domain, Māra’s bait, Māra’s range.
mn106:3.5: They lead to these evil, unskillful mental states: greed, ill will, & contentiousness.
mn106:3.6: They arise for the obstruction of a disciple of the noble ones here in training.
mn106:3.7: What if I—overpowering the world [of the five senses] and having determined my mind—were to dwell with an awareness that was abundant & enlarged?
mn106:3.8: Having done so, these evil, unskillful mental states—greed, ill will, & contentiousness—would not come into being.
mn106:3.9: With their abandoning, my mind would become unlimited, immeasurable, & well developed.’
mn106:3.10: Practicing & frequently abiding in this way, his mind acquires confidence in that dimension.
mn106:3.11: There being full confidence, he either attains the imperturbable now or else is committed to discernment.
mn106:3.12: With the break-up of the body, after death, it’s possible that this leading-on consciousness of his will go to the imperturbable.
mn106:3.13: This is declared to be the first practice conducive to the imperturbable.
mn106:4.1: Then again, the disciple of the noble ones considers this:
mn106:4.2: ‘Sensuality here & now; sensuality in lives to come;
mn106:4.3: sensual perceptions here & now; sensual perceptions in lives to come:
mn106:4.4: Whatever is form, every form, is the four great elements or a form derived from the four great elements.’
mn106:4.5: Practicing & frequently abiding in this way, his mind acquires confidence in that dimension.
mn106:4.6: There being full confidence, he either attains the imperturbable now or else is committed to discernment.
mn106:4.7: With the break-up of the body, after death, it’s possible that this leading-on consciousness of his will go to the imperturbable.
mn106:4.8: This is declared to be the second practice conducive to the imperturbable.
mn106:5.1: Then again, the disciple of the noble ones considers this:
mn106:5.2: ‘Sensuality here & now; sensuality in lives to come;
mn106:5.3: sensual perceptions here & now; sensual perceptions in lives to come;
mn106:5.4: forms here & now; forms in lives to come;
mn106:5.5: form-perceptions here & now; form-perceptions in lives to come:
mn106:5.7: Whatever is inconstant is not worth relishing, is not worth welcoming, is not worth remaining fastened to.’
mn106:5.8: Practicing & frequently abiding in this way, his mind acquires confidence in that dimension.
mn106:5.9: There being full confidence, he either attains the imperturbable now or else is committed to discernment.
mn106:5.10: With the break-up of the body, after death, it’s possible that this leading-on consciousness of his will go to the imperturbable.
mn106:5.11: This is declared to be the third practice conducive to the imperturbable.
mn106:6.1: Then again, the disciple of the noble ones considers this:
mn106:6.2: ‘Sensuality here & now; sensuality in lives to come;
mn106:6.3: sensual perceptions here & now; sensual perceptions in lives to come;
mn106:6.4: forms here & now; forms in lives to come;
mn106:6.5: form-perceptions here & now; form-perceptions in lives to come;
mn106:6.8: Where they cease without remainder: that is peaceful, that is exquisite,
mn106:6.10: Practicing & frequently abiding in this way, his mind acquires confidence in that dimension.
mn106:6.11: There being full confidence, he either attains the dimension of nothingness now or else is committed to discernment.
mn106:6.12: With the break-up of the body, after death, it’s possible that this leading-on consciousness of his will go to the dimension of nothingness.
mn106:6.13: This is declared to be the first practice conducive to the dimension of nothingness.
mn106:7.1: Then again, the disciple of the noble ones, having gone into the wilderness, to the root of a tree, or into an empty dwelling, considers this:
mn106:7.2: ‘This is empty of self or of anything pertaining to self.’
mn106:7.3: Practicing & frequently abiding in this way, his mind acquires confidence in that dimension.
mn106:7.4: There being full confidence, he either attains the dimension of nothingness now or else is committed to discernment.
mn106:7.5: With the break-up of the body, after death, it’s possible that this leading-on consciousness of his will go to the dimension of nothingness.
mn106:7.6: This is declared to be the second practice conducive to the dimension of nothingness.
mn106:8.1: Then again, the disciple of the noble ones considers this:
mn106:8.2: ‘I am not anyone’s anything anywhere; nor is anything of mine in anyone anywhere.’
mn106:8.3: Practicing & frequently abiding in this way, his mind acquires confidence in that dimension.
mn106:8.4: There being full confidence, he either attains the dimension of nothingness now or else is committed to discernment.
mn106:8.5: With the break-up of the body, after death, it’s possible that this leading-on consciousness of his will go to the dimension of nothingness.
mn106:8.6: This is declared to be the third practice conducive to the dimension of nothingness.
mn106:9.1: Then again, the disciple of the noble ones considers this:
mn106:9.2: ‘Sensuality here & now; sensuality in lives to come;
mn106:9.3: sensual perceptions here & now; sensual perceptions in lives to come;
mn106:9.4: forms here & now; forms in lives to come;
mn106:9.5: form-perceptions here & now; form-perceptions in lives to come;
mn106:9.6: perceptions of the imperturbable; perceptions of the dimension of nothingness:
mn106:9.8: Where they cease without remainder: That is peaceful, that is exquisite,
mn106:9.9: i.e., the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception.’
mn106:9.10: Practicing & frequently abiding in this way, his mind acquires confidence in that dimension.
mn106:9.11: There being full confidence, he either attains the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception now or else is committed to discernment.
mn106:9.12: With the break-up of the body, after death, it’s possible that this leading-on consciousness of his will go to the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception.
mn106:9.13: This is declared to be the practice conducive to the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception.”
mn106:10.1: When this was said, Ven. Ānanda said to the Blessed One:
mn106:10.2: “There is the case, lord, where a monk, having practiced in this way—
mn106:10.3: ‘It should not be, it should not occur to me; it will not be, it will not occur to me. What is, what has come to be, that I abandon’—
mn106:10.5: Now, would this monk be totally unbound, or not?”
mn106:10.6: “A certain such monk might, Ānanda, and another might not.’
mn106:10.7: “What is the cause, what is the reason, lord, whereby one might and another might not?”
mn106:10.8: “There is the case, Ānanda, where a monk, having practiced in this way—(thinking)
mn106:10.9: ‘It should not be, it should not occur to me; it will not be, it will not occur to me. What is, what has come to be, that I abandon’—
mn106:10.11: He relishes that equanimity, welcomes it, remains fastened to it.
mn106:10.12: As he relishes that equanimity, welcomes it, remains fastened to it, his consciousness is dependent on it, is sustained by it [clings to it].
mn106:10.13: With clinging/sustenance, Ānanda, a monk is not totally unbound.”
mn106:11.1: “Being sustained, lord, where is that monk sustained?”
mn106:11.2: “The dimension of neither perception nor non-perception.”
mn106:11.3: “Then, indeed, being sustained, he is sustained by the supreme sustenance.”
mn106:11.4: “Being sustained, Ānanda, he is sustained by the supreme sustenance;
mn106:11.5: for this—the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception—
mn106:12.1: There is (however) the case where a monk, having practiced in this way—
mn106:12.2: ‘It should not be, it should not occur to me; it will not be, it will not occur to me. What is, what has come to be, that I abandon’—
mn106:12.4: He does not relish that equanimity, does not welcome it, does not remain fastened to it.
mn106:12.5: As he does not relish that equanimity, does not welcome it, does not remain fastened to it, his consciousness is not dependent on it, is not sustained by it [does not cling to it].
mn106:12.6: Without clinging/sustenance, Ānanda, a monk is totally unbound.”
mn106:13.2: For truly, the Blessed One has declared to us the way to cross over the flood by going from one support to the next.
mn106:13.3: But what is the noble liberation?”
mn106:13.4: “There is the case, Ānanda, where a disciple of the noble ones considers this:
mn106:13.5: ‘Sensuality here & now; sensuality in lives to come;
mn106:13.6: sensual perceptions here & now; sensual perceptions in lives to come;
mn106:13.7: forms here & now; forms in lives to come;
mn106:13.8: form-perceptions here & now; form-perceptions in lives to come;
mn106:13.9: perceptions of the imperturbable; perceptions of the dimension of nothingness; perceptions of the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception:
mn106:13.10: That is an identity, to the extent that there is an identity.
mn106:13.11: This is deathless: the liberation of the mind through lack of clinging/sustenance.’
mn106:14.1: Now, Ānanda, I have taught the practice conducive to the imperturbable. I have taught the practice conducive to the dimension of nothingness. I have taught the practice conducive to the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception. I have taught the way to cross over the flood by going from one support to the next, the noble liberation.
mn106:15.1: Whatever a teacher should do—seeking the welfare of his disciples, out of sympathy for them—that have I done for you.
mn106:15.2: Over there are the roots of trees; over there, empty dwellings. Practice jhāna, Ānanda. Don’t be heedless. Don’t later fall into remorse. That is our message to you all.”
mn106:15.3: That is what the Blessed One said.
mn106:15.4: Gratified, Ven. Ānanda delighted in the Blessed One’s words.
